That is to say, of the $625B in MSFT’s remaining performance obligations, $281B have the name OpenAI, a company that is not profitable, that is projecting multi-billion losses, and that is dependent on funding rounds that keep happening. So for me, this is a risk to take into account because it is not only the risk of concentration in a client that burns cash at high speed, but it is also actively diversifying with competitors.

Does CapEx justify the current multiple?

If we put on the bull hat, we could talk about Jevons paradox, meaning that when inference costs fall, total compute demand rises because cheaper models enable use cases that previously had no economic viability. And this can be stated knowing that Gemini 3 Pro processes 3x more daily tokens than its predecessor despite a 78% drop in serving costs, and with data center utilization projected to exceed 95% by the end of 2026.

Now then, at the opposite extreme, we have, for example, that UBS (UBS) estimates that 2026 CapEx of the hyperscalers will consume practically 100% of operating cash flow. Considering that we come from 40%, this is something worth highlighting. In addition, we have an interesting study from Princeton that points out that the real useful life of chips reaches 1y-3y due to technological obsolescence itself, and that it is more optimistic than what could be reflected in the accounting of hyperscalers. So if we go to the financial statements, and we see that leveraged FCF effectively goes from $61B to $53.6B despite a 17% increase in revenues, I do give credibility to this reasoning.

Year Revenue OCF CapEx CapEx/OCF Levered FCF
FY2021 $168B $76.7B $20.6B 27% $41.3B
FY2022 $198B $89.0B $23.9B 27% $49.5B
FY2023 $212B $87.6B $28.1B 32% $47.3B
FY2024 $245B $118.5B $44.5B 37% $56.7B
FY2025 $282B $136.2B $64.6B 47% $61.1B
TTM $305B $160.5B $83.1B 52% $53.6B

If MSFT has the ability to convert the $120B annual CapEx into revenue before margins erode due to model efficiency, and before OpenAI completes its diversification, it could indeed be something reassuring. But until then, we have to wait for the next earnings release.

What does the price tell us?

This is the point I give the most weight to right now, because I think few people doubt the quality of MSFT and most will agree that in 10 years the price will be higher than this level, but can we avoid getting trapped with the opportunity cost that it would imply?

My answer is yes. A bearish structure is clearly visible on the daily timeframe with the 30 and 200 moving averages above current prices and with a negative slope, with only the MA200 on the weekly timeframe still maintaining a positive slope, although the price is already below it.

The volume traded at these levels also assigns more probability to a $320-$340 scenario, since a fairly relevant historical volume between $395-$480 has also been lost. A zone that I consider as trapped sellers and that will pressure any rebound before it can develop.

Losing the last support located at $365-$370, unless it is defended, the first level is $350, and if the trend continues, $300 would not be something unreasonable either.

I think a great buying opportunity may be forming, but it is not the moment; there is no rush, and I prefer operating in favor of a positive trend.

My takeaway

Is it the moment to build a position in MSFT? For me, the answer is no.

If I look at the more fundamental side first, I think Azure is losing momentum against Google Cloud, with an OpenAI that is actively diversifying into competitors while the company maintains 45% of its RPO in it. In addition, the ROI obtained versus CapEx still cannot be quantified with sufficient precision beyond estimates.

Which leaves us with price analysis as a powerful tool to understand how the market is quantifying these estimates, and right now, since November 2025, the price is telling us that maintaining a cautious position in the stock would be the right approach to protect capital.

My target is $330, and if a trend reversal occurs there, beyond rebounds within a bearish structure, I would then be able to start building a position in the stock again. Until then, monitoring and caution.

data.title: BlackSky's Gen-3 Inflexion Meets High Market Expectations
data.description:
Thesis

BlackSky (BKSY) managed to report a 4Q25 GAAP EPS of -$0.02, a figure that beat expectations by $0.16. Revenue, on the other hand, came in at $35 million, up about 16% since last year but missing estimates by about $2.1 million. The quarter also showed us much-improved profitability, with net losses narrowing and a pretty strong adjusted EBITDA performance. We also saw decent commercial traction, with the backlog jumping 32% to $345 million. What I like about that backlog is that it’s mainly driven by Gen-3 demand, which we want to see. Once a customer gets on board with a Gen-3 subscription, it can be scaled up to a higher margin contract by adding on other services such as AI solutions and ground systems. As for guidance, management is expecting $120 million to $145 million in revenue, which was slightly below the consensus at the midpoint. We can also expect $6 million to $18 million in adjusted EBITDA, and $50 million to $60 million in capex.

In my previous coverage, I gave an in-depth comparison between BlackSky and its similar competitor, Planet Labs (PL). Now, we have just seen Planet deliver stellar earnings with an increasing backlog. It brought Planet's stock up to the $33 range and signalled strong demand for the satellite intelligence space. As for BlackSky, my bull case here would rest on the Gen-3 driving a step-change in demand and then enabling the high-margin, recurring AI and imagery subscriptions that get added to a contract. This should help convert backlog into accelerating revenue growth. And, if execution holds, operating leverage from a scaled constellation could push the business toward more durable profitability. This is going to be much needed to justify its premium valuation, as I'll explain.

FY25 review

What we wanted to see from BlackSky’s FY25 earnings was less early-stage volatility and more stable, contract-driven growth. That being said, there were some underlying pressure points. Revenue growth was only modest; on a full-year basis, it came in at $106.6 million, just up slightly from $102.1 million in 2024, about 4% growth. But what was more interesting to me was the composition of that revenue since it shifted quite meaningfully. As you probably know, the standout was the mission solutions segment, which we saw jump from $5.9 million to $21.2 million since last year.

So clearly there’s some increasing traction in higher-value, customised contracts tied to defence and intelligence use cases. This is where BlackSky needs to build its revenue foundation from. At the same time, legacy space-based imagery revenue actually declined, which would suggest that BlackSky is somewhat pivoting toward integrated solutions rather than just pure data sales. The big real signal of demand strength here was the 32% backlog increase to $345 million, and the good thing is that it was heavily driven by international Gen-3 contracts.

As for profitability, things were a bit more nuanced. My initial thoughts were that 4Q25 looks strong, with net loss shrinking pretty dramatically to just $0.9 million from $19.4 million, and adjusted EBITDA managing to reach $8.8 million. However, I think much of the net loss improvement was driven mainly by non-operational factors, such as some derivative gains tied to equity instruments, rather than purely better cost efficiency. For FY25, losses actually widened to $70.3 million, and adjusted EBITDA dropped quite sharply from $11.6 million to $0.9 million.

So these were signs of some heavier investment and scaling costs, especially with the cost of sales rising to 33% of revenue. Operating expenses, on the other hand, were flat overall, but cash operating expenses did increase a bit. So there’s a real underlying spend growth despite their accounting adjustments. The balance sheet actually improved in liquidity terms, with cash and investments up to about $126 million. And yes, this was mainly due to financing activity. This cash jump also came alongside a significant increase in long-term debt, which is now up to $193 million, so there’s clearly a big reliance on external capital to fund satellite deployments, since the Gen-3 is still being launched.

In terms of guidance, management is expecting $120 million to $145 million in revenue and up to $18 million in adjusted EBITDA. So based on that, we can expect some acceleration driven by Gen-3 deployment and backlog conversion. But the key question for me here is whether BlackSky can actually translate strong contract momentum into consistent, high-margin recurring revenue while also controlling capital intensity. This is the challenge, especially with capital expenditures expected to rise to $50 million to $60 million.

Backlog conversion

So a big driver of the growth story here is going to be that increasing backlog. We would expect that this should improve revenue visibility, and it seems that management also thinks this is the case. BlackSky managed to end FY25 with a $345 million backlog, up 32% year-over-year, which was driven by about $240 million in new contract bookings, which was nice to see. Also, the majority of them are supposedly multi-year and increasingly international. Importantly here, management indicated that about $75 million of this backlog is already expected to convert into FY26 revenue, so that's already a meaningful baseline against their $120 million to $145 million guidance.

However, we have to keep in mind here the quality of this visibility, in that it needs to be understood correctly. You see, much of the backlog is tied to mission solutions contracts and sovereign deals. As we know, the revenue for such contracts is inherently lumpy in both timing and revenue recognition simply due to the nature of the milestones. So we can expect these contracts to generate large revenue spikes when milestones are achieved, or hardware is delivered, rather than smooth quarterly flows. So, reported growth may continue to look uneven across the quarters in 2026 despite underlying demand strength.

At the same time, we’re seeing BlackSky try to somewhat reshape its business model into a hybrid structure, in that they have a decent blend of recurring software-like revenue, but also large, project-based contracts. Management is now framing operations across three segments. The first is space-based intelligence and AI services, which make up about 60% to 70% of revenue. This is the core, higher-margin subscription business, so that's when a client wants imagery and coverage along with tools like Spectre. These are high-margin, as BlackSky already has most of the satellite framework in place, operating at high coverage and revisit rates. So this is quick to scale and very sticky since once a company integrates Gen-3’s imagery services into their operations, it can be very hard to switch. The second segment is mission solutions, which accounts for about 25% of revenue. It includes things like satellite sales, ground systems, and some other integration work. Lastly, there are advanced technology programs making up about 15% and consisting of customer-funded R&D.

A Gen-3 online in under a week

In mid-March, we also got the news of a key operational milestone for the Gen-3 rollout. The fourth satellite was actually commissioned in under one week post-launch, which is extremely fast. Much faster than the industry norm of several months, which goes to show you just how advanced the Gen-3 really is. And because it is now operational already, it immediately unlocks that full commercial availability. So, 35 cm high-resolution imagery and AI-enabled analytics across the global customer base via the Spectra platform. Another satellite up will effectively increase usable capacity but should also accelerate time-to-revenue. I think the ability to bring satellites online this quickly can't be understated here. It not only extends asset lifespan and ROI, but also strengthens BlackSky’s positioning in real-time. So those who want extremely low revisit rates of under an hour, as we usually see in defence applications, should get that as well as high-frequency monitoring.

Now, this strategic shift should see all these bundled together, with mission solutions deals such as getting a customer on board with a Gen-3 subscription as an entry point. The plan from there would then be to attach long-term contracts for imagery, analytics, and AI-driven insights. It’s a somewhat land-and-expand dynamic where upfront hardware revenue, such as the Gen-3 imagery, is then followed by recurring, higher-margin service revenue. Over time, if executed well, I think this model could increase revenue durability and margins. However, in the near term, it also reinforces the mixed revenue profile here, where predictable subscriptions are still getting partially offset by the timing volatility of those large contract wins.

Valuation

When it comes to a valuation, it seems BlackSky is being priced a lot like a high-growth, strategic space/data platform rather than a company with $107 million in annual revenue and ongoing losses. The current EV-to-sales ratio is at 10.6x on a trailing basis and about 8.6x on a forward basis. Elsewhere, the price-to-sales is 8.9x trailing. Both of these multiples sit roughly 3x to 4x above the sector medians. Even within the growth-oriented aerospace or defence-tech peers, that double-digit EV to sales would typically imply expectations of sustained high growth, improving margins, and strong competitive positioning, not just incremental expansion. There’s also a very elevated EV to EBITDA forward multiple at 91x, which again shows that disconnect between current profitability and valuation. The market here is clearly largely looking past near-term earnings.

What I think is being priced in here is a successful scaling of the Gen-3 platform into a high-margin, recurring revenue model. This should ideally also come with some meaningful operating leverage over time. The premium would also suggest expectations of accelerating revenue growth, well above the current 4% pace we’re seeing. So going forward, strong backlog conversion and a transition toward durable double-digit EBITDA margins are a must to justify those multiples. It would also reflect a belief that BlackSky can carve out a pretty defensible niche in real-time geospatial intelligence, given their AI advancements and coverage with the Gen-3. So in that sense, we would like to see increasing global defence demand acting as a tailwind. In saying that, this valuation obviously leaves little room for execution missteps; if growth stalls or even if we see capital intensity remain high, the multiples could compress significantly.

Looking ahead

So in 2026, I feel the story will hinge on execution against the backlog. Gen-3 deployment is key, in which we're already seeing good signs with services becoming online for the latest launch in under a week. This should pick up the pace of conversion into recurring revenue. Results will likely remain uneven simply due to the nature of their contract timing and continued heavy investment. The key markers I'll be watching are consistent subscription growth, margin expansion, and some signs of operating leverage as more Gen-3 capacity comes online.

So just to sum up, the bull case still remains intact in that BlackSky is positioning itself as a high-value, real-time geospatial intelligence platform. The Gen-3 is the catalyst here for scaling into higher-margin, recurring AI-driven revenue. And if the company can successfully convert that backlog into revenue while still controlling capital intensity as launches scale down, there is definitely a path toward sustainable profitability and valuation support here. But execution will be everything.

data.title: Federal Realty: A Dividend King Built For Market Volatility
data.description:
Market volatility is back in full swing, with fear now ruling the market, given uncertainties around AI disruption and higher oil prices due to the Iran war. That’s why it’s now more important than ever to focus on quality companies with durable income streams. Dividends and quality assets can greatly help to guard against portfolio losses.

This brings me to Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT), which is a Dividend King that has weathered countless macroeconomic events over its 50+ consecutive years of increasing payouts. I last covered FRT back in October 2025, highlighting its premier portfolio with robust leasing and strong rent growth.

My investment thesis appears to have been validated by the 4.5% total return since my last piece, far outpacing the 5.4% decline in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe. At the current price of $103, FRT trades comfortably below its 52-week high of $111 with a 4.4% dividend yield.

In this article, I revisit FRT including recent business results, and discuss why it’s currently a sound ‘buy’ for safety, value, and income, so let’s get started!

Why FRT?

Federal Realty Investment Trust is a retail REIT that owns high-quality open-air shopping centers and mixed-use properties in affluent, supply-constrained markets across the U.S.

FRT’s strategy centers around “dominant” assets in strong suburban locations, often complemented by residential and office components that enhance traffic and long-term value. This is supported by favorable population demographics, with an average 3-mile radius household income of $167K. It also happens to have a 3-mile average population of 167K.

As shown below, FRT’s 3-mile population density and median household income compare favorably to those of its open-air shopping center peers like Regency Centers (REG) and Kimco Realty (KIM).

At present, FRT owns 103 open-air properties covering 29 million square feet of commercial space. It has a diverse tenant base numbering 3,700 and has 2,500 residential units. 80% of FRT’s ABR (annual base rent) comes from retail, and the remainder is evenly split between residential and mixed-use office. As shown below, top tenants include TJX (TJX), NetApp (NTAP), Cisco (CSCO), Gap (GAP), and Amazon (AMZN).

FRT delivered solid Q4 2025 results, with core FFO per share growing by 6.4% YoY, marking an acceleration from full-year growth of 4.3%. The portfolio is healthy with a 96.1% leased rate. FRT had a record-breaking 2025, with an all-time company high leasing volume of 2.5 million square feet of retail space.

Also encouraging, FRT achieved the strongest rent spreads in over a decade at 15% and 27% on a cash and straight-line basis, respectively. This reflects the strong embedded mark-to-market growth in FRT’s portfolio as new leases are being signed at materially higher starting rents than prior levels.

Looking ahead, management is guiding for full-year core FFO/share growth of 6%. Comparable property income growth is expected to be in the 3% to 3.5% range. This is down slightly from last year due to temporary occupancy drag from anchor tenant transitions.

Meanwhile, industry dynamics support FRT as there is limited new retail supply combined with resilient consumer spending in higher-income regions. Plus, FRT’s acquisition and mixed-use development strategy are also expected to add value. New projects are expected to deliver yields in the 6.5% to 7% range. These include developments in Bala Cynwyd, Hoboken, and Santana Row, which will add 500+ residential units.

FRT also has opportunities to recycle capital to juice growth. This is reflected by recent sales of non-core properties with low 5% cap rates while acquiring assets with targeted internal rates of return approaching 9% through merchandising and rent growth. The following example at the Leawood, KS property shows FRT’s ability to attract vibrant tenants like lululemon (LULU) and Coach (TPR) with a targeted 9% 10-year IRR.

FRT carries a strong balance sheet with a BBB+ credit rating from S&P. It has $1.3 billion of liquidity and a safe net debt to EBITDA ratio of 5.6x, sitting just above its long-term target of 5.5x. Plus, 83% of FRT’s debt is fixed rate, and it has a solid fixed charge coverage ratio of 3.9x. This supports FRT’s 4.4% dividend yield, which is well covered by a 61% payout ratio. FRT is also a Dividend King with 57 consecutive years of annual raises.

I continue to find value in FRT at the current price of $102.87 with a forward P/FFO of 13.75, sitting far below its historical P/FFO of 21.1, as shown below.

At the current price, I believe the market isn’t giving FRT sufficient credit for its high-quality asset base, accelerating fundamentals, and strong forward outlook. I believe FRT could reasonably sustain 5-6% annual FFO/share growth through its mark-to-market rent growth and developments. This, combined with the 4.4% dividend, could lead to total annual returns of around 10%. While this isn’t an eye-popping return, it matches the long-term expected return of the overall market with a far higher dividend yield and a premier asset base.

Risks to FRT include the potential for higher interest rates, as that could raise its cost of debt funding for mixed-use developments. Also, FRT is more dependent on high-end consumer spending compared. This means that its centers are less necessity-based (i.e. grocery) and a pullback in spending from its demographic group could result in lower rent growth.

Investor Takeaway

Federal Realty Investment Trust stands out as a high-quality REIT that’s well positioned to navigate today’s volatile market environment. Its portfolio of dominant, affluent-market retail and mixed-use assets continues to drive strong leasing spreads, steady occupancy, and mid-single-digit FFO growth.

FRT’s conservative balance sheet and 57-year dividend growth streak reinforce its reliability. Despite these strengths, FRT trades at a meaningful discount to its historical valuation. Meanwhile, it offers a 4.4% yield with room for continued growth both organically and through its development pipeline.

This sets up potential 10% total returns annually from this Dividend King while giving stability in a volatile market. For these reasons, I maintain a ‘Buy’ rating on FRT.

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 2029-2032 Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary1,2 - Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500 Index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large caps had only a slight advantage over small caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented healthcare sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI. All other groups lagged the index. Notably, IT, which represented 35% of the S&P 500 Index in Q4, rose about 1%.

International developed-market stocks advanced 4.89%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index. The discrepancy between value and growth stocks was more pronounced in this index, again in favor of value over growth. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 4.72% rise. South Korea (+27%) and Taiwan (+10%) were two strong components with sizable representation in this index, bolstered by the strength in technology stocks in those markets. On the other hand, China finished the quarter with a roughly -7% return, giving back some of its gains since the April bottom.

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.1% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals.

The U.S. equity bull market is entering its fourth year, having put up double-digit gains in each of the past three, leaving investors wondering whether it can keep the streak alive. While multiyear stretches of positive equity returns are common, double-digit returns four years in row are few and far between. The naysayers point to elevated valuations, narrowness of the market, uncertainty around Fed policy amid upcoming leadership change, a softening labor market, and U.S. mid-term election risk. Yet, history has shown that bull markets don't die of old age alone, and while valuations are elevated, they're below prior cycle extremes. Bulls can also point to still-robust earnings expectations, a broadening of AI beneficiaries, fiscal support, increased capital spending, deregulation, rising mergers-and-acquisitions activity, and easing trade tensions. With the risks reasonably balanced and momentum still intact, the path of least resistance for the equity market may continue to be higher for now. The question is, can it reach double digits for the four-peat?

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a single-digit gain for the quarter and trailed its blended benchmark, comprising a 35.87% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index, a 15.38% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net) and a 48.75% weighting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Disciplined Value International Fund (John Hancock) was a noteworthy detractor for the quarter. The fund's lower allocation to value stocks and higher allocation to growth versus the MSCI EAFE Value Index was a headwind in a quarter where international value equities strongly outperformed growth names. Additionally, the portfolio's exposure to a hedged equity strategy detracted, as stock indexes moved higher, closing out a strong year. Conversely, International Growth Fund (John Hancock) contributed on a relative basis, largely due to security selection in the financials sector. U.S. Growth Fund (John Hancock) also contributed, aided by stock selection in IT and industrials. A tactical overweight to international value equities further contributed, given the relatively strong performance of that group.

This commentary reflects the views of the portfolio managers named and is subject to change as market and other conditions warrant. No forecasts are guaranteed. This commentary is provided for informational purposes only and is not an endorsement of any security, mutual fund, sector, or index. The subadvisor, the advisor (John Hancock Investment Management LLC), and their affiliates, employees, and clients, may hold or trade the securities mentioned in this commentary. data.title: Meta: I'm More Than Willing To Catch The Falling Knife Now
data.description:
Meta: Deep Into A Bear Market, But Justified?

The bear market in the stocks of Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) is simply getting from bad to worse. Down almost 35% from the 2025 highs, I wouldn't blame you if you're starting to question whether this recovery will even pan out, because it seems like Meta management is not deterred.

In fact, despite reportedly facing challenges on its upcoming launch of its most updated and advanced AI model (Avocado), the company has doubled down on several deals recently, ranging from securing more AI compute capacity, to striking AI chip partnerships with AMD (AMD) and Arm (ARM). If anything, I think Meta is demonstrating just how ambitious and confident it is on its AI roadmap, and wouldn't be cajoled into giving up on its multi-year journey right now, just as its archrivals have also ramped up their pressure, including Microsoft (MSFT), as the software leader has also reorganized its ranks to focus on better consumer and commercial integration, while also concentrating on achieving superintelligence.

In other words, I think Meta can no longer hide behind the promises of being in the leadership position of attaining superintelligence ahead of others, particularly as it faces more snags on its next gen AI models. We will have to wait until May before we can assess and get our hands dirty on what management has in store for us while comparing its advances and developments to those of the AI labs. Even OpenAI (OPENAI) is facing tremendous pressure right now, as Anthropic (ANTHRO) has proven more than an even match against the Sam Altman-led company. Anthropic has arguably earned the trust and confidence of the AI industry, as it continues to launch industry leading, and cutting edge AI coding tools, as the market relishes the power and productivity boosts from the agentic capabilities driven by its AI models.

Meta's AI Ambitions Not Given Enough Respect Yet

Whereas for Meta, I guess we could very well say that the company has still not been able to fully convince us that it has what it takes to not just keep up with the rest of its mega-tech peers, but also justify valuation multiples that suggest the company belongs in the pantheon of AI greats. Investors want to know that AI isn't just benefiting its advertising-driven business model, but also one that befits the reputation of a world leading AI lab, given how it snagged the spotlight with the eye popping compensation deals it offered as it amassed some of the most precocious talents in the AI field. Therefore, I think it's only justified for us to want to see much better execution under the leadership of its chief AI officer, Alex Wang, and the hype that Meta has driven.

More importantly, I believe many wouldn't disagree with me that without a highly profitable and fast growing cloud computing business model akin to Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), the company's advertising heavy growth is more susceptible to unanticipated market volatility or economic cycles, and even potential legal ramifications that may emanate from its recent landmark suit in the social media case, as its algorithm went on trial for " addictive behavior" and lost.

Of course we know that the legal process is far from over, and I believe that Meta Platforms will almost definitely be fighting back vehemently in subsequent appeal processes. So, a potential multi-year legal fight could happen, and plausibly spur an unwelcome hangover that may persist for the foreseeable future, given how reliant Meta is on advertising to drive profits and validate its intense capital expenditure spending on the AI infrastructure buildout.

When I updated my buy rating on META in my last write-up, I didn't quite expect the stock to take such a significant tumble, and now it is inching closer to the April 2025 lows. I knew that the market would look toward management to provide more proof points for us to corroborate that the AI spending is sustainable. However, the fact is that, while Meta has gone on to establish bigger and bolder partnerships with several partners, it hasn’t quite earned the confidence of investors, other than making big splashes through its spending abilities to illustrate that it could well afford to spend. On any other day, I thought these partnerships should have impressed the market about Meta’s ambitions. And to be brutally honest, these arrangements and partnerships are not immaterial, and are significant by most measures. These include:

  • The $27 billion multi-year commitment to “access advanced AI infrastructure from Nebius (NBIS).”
  • Meta upgraded the spending on the data center campus in El Paso, Texas, to $10 billion, from an initial $1 billion, lifting it toward a 1 GW capacity by 2028.
  • Meta is also working in closer collaboration with Entergy (ETR) to make sure that it has the necessary power for the “massive data center it is developing in rural Louisiana.” Key to note is that the arrangement was “structured to ensure Meta pays its full cost of service."

But, the lack of bullish follow-through suggests that the market is looking beyond big spending promises right now to prove to the world that Meta can deliver AI monetization beyond just advertising, and into future revenue streams.

Why is that so? Meta looked to “shock” the world, as it unveiled a revised executive compensation plan that would reward some of its most senior executives with stock options that will require META stock to hit a “market value of at least $9.6 trillion” in the next 5 years.

Meta Has Proven Its Execution Before. It Could Do It Once Again

This is obviously a monumental show of force, I believe, from Mark Zuckerberg to remind the world to keep our faith. Zuckerberg obviously believes that Meta will ultimately deliver the goods and the shareholder value through the rest of the decade. Yet, questions have arisen linked to the aggressive AI spending outlay without a concomitant cloud business to help offset these costs. Hence, it's not unreasonable that some of us could contend such outlays are possibly profligate by some corners of the market.

And if we're not careful, Meta’s ad business could also be caught in the legal maelstrom that could lead to unforeseen changes to its AI algorithm and its advertising models that have been so beneficial in driving recent engagement growth, validating the company's decision to spend even more than before. Yet, the observation from Wall Street is that, unless Meta could open up new growth levers beyond advertising, believing that the market will rerate the stock with a much higher multiple may seem too far fetched: “To get to these strike prices means that Meta must create new revenue streams, and it can’t be strictly digital advertising.”

Hence, I don't think any one of us is really surprised that Meta is reportedly looking to reduce headcount to pay for its AI spending, in view of the current circumstances. However, I think the market clearly wants more proof of concept now, and cutting headcount will not be enough, as investors face the pressure of seeing potentially lower free cash flow margins this year, below 5%, even though they are still expected to climb out from the nadir, and recover to the >20% mark (potentially) by 2029. In other words, I don't think the market is expecting more spending promises from Meta without any clear monetization improvements beyond what they are showing right now.

Wall Street is also expecting a 25% growth in revenue this year, so it is not as if the market is expecting things to take a disastrous turn down south. Meta’s advertising pillars are working fine, and they are ramping up well in the AI efforts, even though they don't appear to be validated yet as compared to the AI foundation companies. It's fair to say that Meta’s AI labs will need to convince investors of their AI chops before a much more substantial valuation rerating could occur.

Let's not forget that Meta is still the largest social media company in the world outside of China, and that is a level of scale that is unmatched, particularly as it penetrates deeper into AI e-commerce, while also elevating efforts on AI glasses, making new breakthroughs ahead of its rivals in defining the next computing device beyond the smartphone.

This is a long haul project, and it's not something that I believe the market is placing significant confidence or value on right now. But that also means that the company has various growth optionalities to drive prospects and opportunities beyond digital advertising.

So I think it's too early for us to write off Meta’s ambitions, even as what we have seen so far is more and more spending, but less so from direct or overt monetization. Meta’s efforts in improving cost efficiencies should mitigate some pressure, but are unlikely to have a gargantuan impact, unlike what it managed in 2023 during the year of efficiency.

With the level of AI capital expenditure spending expected to be >$138 billion for FY2027, and >$146 billion for FY2028, it is going to put even more pressure on Meta to overcome the pressures from the more cyclical advertising business. Especially since it appears to be willing to continue spending aggressively in AI not only to keep up with its competitors but also to maintain its AI leadership to secure the most elite talent, and also to fulfill its ambitions of creating a new hardware computing platform for AI in the future, and stretch its revenue beyond advertising, which is likely not priced in yet.

Buy Or Sell META Stock?

With META stock having tumbled deep into a bear market, its valuation multiples have also collapsed. And I don’t think I'm the only one who saw that META is valued at a forward earnings multiple of only 17.4x, which is actually below that of the S&P 500’s roughly 20x, and the Magnificent Seven’s 23x metric.

In other words, the market has done what I think it is built to do, which is to reflect enough pessimism, so that it corresponds to the potential risk Meta is arguably facing right now.

It is not often that we see META being valued at this downcast level over the past five years. The only time it went even worse than the current zone was back in 2022/23, as Meta pulled away from the brink when it embarked on its year of efficiency, cutting costs and determined to make a decisive comeback.

This time around, again the spending ambitions have unnerved the market drastically. However, AI isn’t quite the metaverse theme that stunned the market then, right? If there's one company that has proven its ability to use AI to bolster its engagement metrics, and ROAS, in its bid to justify its AI spend, Meta stands out for me.

So, while the market may be right to reflect higher execution risks, I think the selloff has been taken way too far, and therefore confers on us a fantastic opportunity to return more aggressively at this level.

Rating: Maintain Buy.
